structure_blocks.heading: Buyer's Guide Socioeconomic considerations - Green procurement
heading_path: Buyer's Guide Socioeconomic considerations - Green procurement
structure_blocks.text: Green procurement The greening aspects that you must take into consideration throughout the procurement process are described as follows. Life cycle costing Life cycle costing pertains to all four stages of the procurement process: planning, acquisition, utilization, and disposal of goods and services. The Policy on Green Procurement applies to all federal government procurement activities. It requires that environmental performance considerations be embedded in procurement decisions, in the same manner as price, performance, quality and availability. Standard language for environmentally preferable packaging In order to meet our federal commitments to a more sustainable approach to producing, using and managing plastics, including reducing plastic waste and marine litter, PSPC is taking concrete steps to address packaging waste in procurement. For the procurement of goods that come in packaging, buyers must include standard language on environmentally preferable packaging. Please refer to the table below to select the appropriate options to integrate into your procurement. Option 1: Mandatory requirements language This is the recommended option. Use this option when products are delivered in commercially available packaging. For implementation questions, see Frequently asked questions (FAQ) regarding standard language (accessible only on the Government of Canada network). Table example with colour Procurement document Language to integrate into procurement documents Notice of Proposed Procurement (NPP) and Request for Information (RFI) Green Procurement - Environmentally Preferable Packaging Public Works and Government Services Canada (PWGSC) is committed to supporting the procurement of environmentally preferable plastic products and the reduction of plastic packaging waste, and is taking concrete steps to better manage the use and disposal of plastics in its own operations as per the Policy on Green Procurement , the Greening Government Strategy: A Government of Canada Directive , and the Ocean Plastics Charter . Environmentally preferable packaging specifications are mandatory requirements in this procurement. Request for X (RFx); where X is Standing Offer, Supply Arrangement or Proposal Part 1 of RFx - Section 1.2 Statement of Work or Requirement The Offeror/Contractor must meet the mandatory environmentally preferable packaging specifications as indicated in the Statement of Work (SOW) or Statement of Requirement (SOR). The Offeror/Contractor must ensure that their recyclable packaging is accepted at the local recycling facilities of the delivery location(s) and must verify with the contracting authority whether their reusable packaging can be reused by the end user. The Offeror/Contractor will be responsible for taking back any packaging that cannot be recycled or reused by the end user at no additional cost to Canada. Statement of Work (SOW) or Statement of Requirement (SOR) In accordance with the Policy on Green Procurement and the Greening Government Strategy: A Government of Canada Directive , the Government of Canada is committed to supporting the procurement of environmentally preferable plastic products and the reduction of plastic packaging waste by integrating environmentally preferable packaging specifications in procurement and contract documents. All packaging material related to this requirement must be reusable, returnable or recyclable in accordance with the definitions set forth in Annex X . Excluded material * can also be found in Annex X . The Contractor is responsible for ensuring that their recyclable packaging is accepted at the local recycling facilities of the delivery location(s) and to verify with the contracting authority whether their reusable packaging can be reused by the end user. The Contractor must take back any packaging that cannot be recycled or reused by the end user at no additional cost to Canada. Option 2: Optional requirements language Use Option 2 for requirements where: the delivered products require specialized packaging products are to be delivered in an area with limited recycling options adding a requirement for environmentally preferable packaging could be a major barrier to adequate supplier participation, to accessibility for persons with disabilities, or a risk to health, safety or national security For implementation questions, see Frequently asked questions (FAQ) regarding standard language (accessible only on the Government of Canada network). Table example with colour Procurement document Language to integrate into procurement documents Notice of Proposed Procurement (NPP) and Request for Information (RFI) Green Procurement - Environmentally Preferable Packaging Public Works and Government Services Canada (PWGSC) is committed to promoting the procurement of environmentally preferable plastic products and the reduction of plastic packaging waste as per the Policy on Green Procurement , the Greening Government Strategy: A Government of Canada Directive , and the Ocean Plastics Charter . Offerors/Contractors are encouraged to use environmentally preferable packaging for all packaged goods to be delivered to the end user as part of this procurement. Request for X (RFx); where X is Standing Offer, Supply Arrangement or Proposal Part 1 of RFx - Section 1.2 Statement of Work or Requirement The Offeror/Contractor should meet the environmentally preferable packaging specifications for this procurement, as indicated in the Statement of Work (SOW) or Statement of Requirement (SOR). The Offeror/Contractor should ensure that their recyclable packaging is accepted at the local recycling facilities of the delivery location(s) and should verify with the contracting authority whether their reusable packaging can be reused by the end user. The Offeror/Contractor should be responsible for taking back any packaging that cannot be recycled or reused by the end user at no additional cost to Canada. Statement of Work (SOW) or Statement of Requirement (SOR) In accordance with the Policy on Green Procurement and the Greening Government Strategy: A Government of Canada Directive , the Government of Canada is committed to promoting the procurement of environmentally preferable plastic products and the reduction of plastic packaging waste by considering environmentally preferable packaging specifications in procurement and contracts. All packaging material related to this requirement should be reusable, returnable or recyclable in accordance with the definitions set forth in Annex X . Excluded material * can also be found in Annex X . The Contractor is responsible for ensuring that their recyclable packaging is accepted at the local recycling facilities of the delivery location(s) and to verify with the contracting authority whether their reusable packaging can be reused by the end user. The Contractor must take back any packaging that cannot be recycled or reused by the end user at no additional cost to Canada. * Note on Excluded material: Environmentally preferable material alternatives for packaging tape are not widely available. As a result, packaging tape is excluded from the environmentally preferable packaging specifications until the market has progressed and studies become available to determine otherwise. Green procurement evaluation indicators Environmental evaluation indicators play a crucial role in developing evaluation criteria for green procurement. These indicators include: Environmental certification, as follows: certification through an independent program like the Environmental Choice Program or Energy Star studies on the environmental attributes of the good or service presence of a certified Environmental Product Declaration by bodies like the Canadian Standards Association or Underwriters laboratories of Canada (UL Solutions) or another accredited certification body Energy and resource efficiency, as follows: use of used, remanufactured, rebuilt or refurbished goods and/or materials efficient resource and energy use throughout the product lifecycle waste reduction capabilities features that save energy, water or fuel, such as Power Down Mode measures to extend the product's life, such as reuse, refill, recharge or recondition Recycled content: inclusion of post-consumer recycled content and the types and percentages of recycled materials used. Hazardous replacement, as follows: availability of non-hazardous alternatives or replacements requirement for Safety Data Sheets Performance testing, as follows: feasibility of pre-purchase testing compliance with performance specifications availability of documented past performance, such as environmental performance reports Packaging, as follows: inclusion of language for environmentally preferable packaging in the solicitation minimal packaging to ensure safe delivery options for bulk or concentrated form purchase offeror removal of packaging post-installation use of reusable, recyclable packaging materials returnable to the contractor, or recyclable at delivery location(s) reduction or elimination of single-use plastics On-site waste management, as follows: source separation and recycling of waste on-site use of environmentally certified haulers and sites design of goods or services to minimize waste, such as using biodegradable or reusable dishes Return for disassembly and recycling, as follows: manufacturer policies for product return for recycling local recycling capabilities for the product acceptance of consumables like toner cartridges for recycling ease of disassembly Warranties, as follows: expected lifespan of the product warranty duration and the advisability of purchasing extended warranties Maintenance, as follows: design conducive to easy maintenance and repair availability of reasonably priced maintenance and replacement parts ease and cost-effectiveness of upgrades Environmental attributes of the offeror, as follows: environmental certifications or registrations, such as ISO 14001 history of regulatory environmental compliance. participation in a greenhouse gas (GHG) emission target-setting, reduction and disclosure programs like the GHG emission Net-Zero Challenge Disclosure of greenhouse gas emissions and the setting of reduction targets You must ensure that any procurement over $25 million, including taxes and set-asides induces suppliers to measure and report their greenhouse gas emissions and set a science-based target to cut emissions. This includes contracts, standing offers, supply arrangements, and call-ups. When the Standard on the Disclosure of Greenhouse Gas Emissions and the Setting of Reduction Targets applies, include this language in the solicitation: “This procurement is subject to the Standard on the Disclosure of Greenhouse Gas Emissions and the Setting of Reduction Targets.” You should also consider applying the standard for procurements between $1 million and $25 million, based on market readiness and client input. There are five ways to apply the standard, depending on how ready the market is: Mandatory or point-rated criteria Certification before contract award Conditionally limited solicitation Contract clause requiring future compliance Deliverable in the statement of work For procurements under standing offers and supply arrangements over $25 million, you must choose a pathway and include it at the appropriate stage. For contracts under $5 million issued under these instruments, the standard can be excluded, and no exemption is needed. Amend contracts issued before April 1, 2023, that exceed $25 million and have three or more years remaining to incorporate the standard. You can request an exemption from the standard if there's a risk of no offers or if it's not feasible. Exemptions must be approved by the responsible senior official and recorded in the file. Offerors can show compliance by participating in the Net-Zero Challenge or an equivalent initiative .

structure_blocks.text: You must ensure that any procurement over $25 million, including taxes and set-asides induces suppliers to measure and report their greenhouse gas emissions and set a science-based target to cut emissions. This includes contracts, standing offers, supply arrangements, and call-ups. When the Standard on the Disclosure of Greenhouse Gas Emissions and the Setting of Reduction Targets applies, include this language in the solicitation:

structure_blocks.heading: Disclosure of greenhouse gas emissions and the setting of reduction targets
heading_path: Buyer's Guide Socioeconomic considerations - Green procurement
Disclosure of greenhouse gas emissions and the setting of reduction targets
structure_blocks.text: “This procurement is subject to the Standard on the Disclosure of Greenhouse Gas Emissions and the Setting of Reduction Targets.”

raw_text:
Green procurement The greening aspects that you must take into consideration throughout the procurement process are described as follows. Life cycle costing Life cycle costing pertains to all four stages of the procurement process: planning, acquisition, utilization, and disposal of goods and services. The Policy on Green Procurement applies to all federal government procurement activities. It requires that environmental performance considerations be embedded in procurement decisions, in the same manner as price, performance, quality and availability. Standard language for environmentally preferable packaging In order to meet our federal commitments to a more sustainable approach to producing, using and managing plastics, including reducing plastic waste and marine litter, PSPC is taking concrete steps to address packaging waste in procurement. For the procurement of goods that come in packaging, buyers must include standard language on environmentally preferable packaging. Please refer to the table below to select the appropriate options to integrate into your procurement. Option 1: Mandatory requirements language This is the recommended option. Use this option when products are delivered in commercially available packaging. For implementation questions, see Frequently asked questions (FAQ) regarding standard language (accessible only on the Government of Canada network). Table example with colour Procurement document Language to integrate into procurement documents Notice of Proposed Procurement (NPP) and Request for Information (RFI) Green Procurement - Environmentally Preferable Packaging Public Works and Government Services Canada (PWGSC) is committed to supporting the procurement of environmentally preferable plastic products and the reduction of plastic packaging waste, and is taking concrete steps to better manage the use and disposal of plastics in its own operations as per the Policy on Green Procurement , the Greening Government Strategy: A Government of Canada Directive , and the Ocean Plastics Charter . Environmentally preferable packaging specifications are mandatory requirements in this procurement. Request for X (RFx); where X is Standing Offer, Supply Arrangement or Proposal Part 1 of RFx - Section 1.2 Statement of Work or Requirement The Offeror/Contractor must meet the mandatory environmentally preferable packaging specifications as indicated in the Statement of Work (SOW) or Statement of Requirement (SOR). The Offeror/Contractor must ensure that their recyclable packaging is accepted at the local recycling facilities of the delivery location(s) and must verify with the contracting authority whether their reusable packaging can be reused by the end user. The Offeror/Contractor will be responsible for taking back any packaging that cannot be recycled or reused by the end user at no additional cost to Canada. Statement of Work (SOW) or Statement of Requirement (SOR) In accordance with the Policy on Green Procurement and the Greening Government Strategy: A Government of Canada Directive , the Government of Canada is committed to supporting the procurement of environmentally preferable plastic products and the reduction of plastic packaging waste by integrating environmentally preferable packaging specifications in procurement and contract documents. All packaging material related to this requirement must be reusable, returnable or recyclable in accordance with the definitions set forth in Annex X . Excluded material * can also be found in Annex X . The Contractor is responsible for ensuring that their recyclable packaging is accepted at the local recycling facilities of the delivery location(s) and to verify with the contracting authority whether their reusable packaging can be reused by the end user. The Contractor must take back any packaging that cannot be recycled or reused by the end user at no additional cost to Canada. Option 2: Optional requirements language Use Option 2 for requirements where: the delivered products require specialized packaging products are to be delivered in an area with limited recycling options adding a requirement for environmentally preferable packaging could be a major barrier to adequate supplier participation, to accessibility for persons with disabilities, or a risk to health, safety or national security For implementation questions, see Frequently asked questions (FAQ) regarding standard language (accessible only on the Government of Canada network). Table example with colour Procurement document Language to integrate into procurement documents Notice of Proposed Procurement (NPP) and Request for Information (RFI) Green Procurement - Environmentally Preferable Packaging Public Works and Government Services Canada (PWGSC) is committed to promoting the procurement of environmentally preferable plastic products and the reduction of plastic packaging waste as per the Policy on Green Procurement , the Greening Government Strategy: A Government of Canada Directive , and the Ocean Plastics Charter . Offerors/Contractors are encouraged to use environmentally preferable packaging for all packaged goods to be delivered to the end user as part of this procurement. Request for X (RFx); where X is Standing Offer, Supply Arrangement or Proposal Part 1 of RFx - Section 1.2 Statement of Work or Requirement The Offeror/Contractor should meet the environmentally preferable packaging specifications for this procurement, as indicated in the Statement of Work (SOW) or Statement of Requirement (SOR). The Offeror/Contractor should ensure that their recyclable packaging is accepted at the local recycling facilities of the delivery location(s) and should verify with the contracting authority whether their reusable packaging can be reused by the end user. The Offeror/Contractor should be responsible for taking back any packaging that cannot be recycled or reused by the end user at no additional cost to Canada. Statement of Work (SOW) or Statement of Requirement (SOR) In accordance with the Policy on Green Procurement and the Greening Government Strategy: A Government of Canada Directive , the Government of Canada is committed to promoting the procurement of environmentally preferable plastic products and the reduction of plastic packaging waste by considering environmentally preferable packaging specifications in procurement and contracts. All packaging material related to this requirement should be reusable, returnable or recyclable in accordance with the definitions set forth in Annex X . Excluded material * can also be found in Annex X . The Contractor is responsible for ensuring that their recyclable packaging is accepted at the local recycling facilities of the delivery location(s) and to verify with the contracting authority whether their reusable packaging can be reused by the end user. The Contractor must take back any packaging that cannot be recycled or reused by the end user at no additional cost to Canada. * Note on Excluded material: Environmentally preferable material alternatives for packaging tape are not widely available. As a result, packaging tape is excluded from the environmentally preferable packaging specifications until the market has progressed and studies become available to determine otherwise. Green procurement evaluation indicators Environmental evaluation indicators play a crucial role in developing evaluation criteria for green procurement. These indicators include: Environmental certification, as follows: certification through an independent program like the Environmental Choice Program or Energy Star studies on the environmental attributes of the good or service presence of a certified Environmental Product Declaration by bodies like the Canadian Standards Association or Underwriters laboratories of Canada (UL Solutions) or another accredited certification body Energy and resource efficiency, as follows: use of used, remanufactured, rebuilt or refurbished goods and/or materials efficient resource and energy use throughout the product lifecycle waste reduction capabilities features that save energy, water or fuel, such as Power Down Mode measures to extend the product's life, such as reuse, refill, recharge or recondition Recycled content: inclusion of post-consumer recycled content and the types and percentages of recycled materials used. Hazardous replacement, as follows: availability of non-hazardous alternatives or replacements requirement for Safety Data Sheets Performance testing, as follows: feasibility of pre-purchase testing compliance with performance specifications availability of documented past performance, such as environmental performance reports Packaging, as follows: inclusion of language for environmentally preferable packaging in the solicitation minimal packaging to ensure safe delivery options for bulk or concentrated form purchase offeror removal of packaging post-installation use of reusable, recyclable packaging materials returnable to the contractor, or recyclable at delivery location(s) reduction or elimination of single-use plastics On-site waste management, as follows: source separation and recycling of waste on-site use of environmentally certified haulers and sites design of goods or services to minimize waste, such as using biodegradable or reusable dishes Return for disassembly and recycling, as follows: manufacturer policies for product return for recycling local recycling capabilities for the product acceptance of consumables like toner cartridges for recycling ease of disassembly Warranties, as follows: expected lifespan of the product warranty duration and the advisability of purchasing extended warranties Maintenance, as follows: design conducive to easy maintenance and repair availability of reasonably priced maintenance and replacement parts ease and cost-effectiveness of upgrades Environmental attributes of the offeror, as follows: environmental certifications or registrations, such as ISO 14001 history of regulatory environmental compliance. participation in a greenhouse gas (GHG) emission target-setting, reduction and disclosure programs like the GHG emission Net-Zero Challenge Disclosure of greenhouse gas emissions and the setting of reduction targets You must ensure that any procurement over $25 million, including taxes and set-asides induces suppliers to measure and report their greenhouse gas emissions and set a science-based target to cut emissions. This includes contracts, standing offers, supply arrangements, and call-ups. When the Standard on the Disclosure of Greenhouse Gas Emissions and the Setting of Reduction Targets applies, include this language in the solicitation: “This procurement is subject to the Standard on the Disclosure of Greenhouse Gas Emissions and the Setting of Reduction Targets.” You should also consider applying the standard for procurements between $1 million and $25 million, based on market readiness and client input. There are five ways to apply the standard, depending on how ready the market is: Mandatory or point-rated criteria Certification before contract award Conditionally limited solicitation Contract clause requiring future compliance Deliverable in the statement of work For procurements under standing offers and supply arrangements over $25 million, you must choose a pathway and include it at the appropriate stage. For contracts under $5 million issued under these instruments, the standard can be excluded, and no exemption is needed. Amend contracts issued before April 1, 2023, that exceed $25 million and have three or more years remaining to incorporate the standard. You can request an exemption from the standard if there's a risk of no offers or if it's not feasible. Exemptions must be approved by the responsible senior official and recorded in the file. Offerors can show compliance by participating in the Net-Zero Challenge or an equivalent initiative .

Green procurement

The greening aspects that you must take into consideration throughout the procurement process are described as follows.

Life cycle costing

Life cycle costing pertains to all four stages of the procurement process: planning, acquisition, utilization, and disposal of goods and services. The Policy on Green Procurement applies to all federal government procurement activities. It requires that environmental performance considerations be embedded in procurement decisions, in the same manner as price, performance, quality and availability.

Standard language for environmentally preferable packaging

In order to meet our federal commitments to a more sustainable approach to producing, using and managing plastics, including reducing plastic waste and marine litter, PSPC is taking concrete steps to address packaging waste in procurement.

For the procurement of goods that come in packaging, buyers must include standard language on environmentally preferable packaging. Please refer to the table below to select the appropriate options to integrate into your procurement.

Option 1: Mandatory requirements language

This is the recommended option. Use this option when products are delivered in commercially available packaging. For implementation questions, see Frequently asked questions (FAQ) regarding standard language (accessible only on the Government of Canada network).

Table example with colour Procurement document Language to integrate into procurement documents Notice of Proposed Procurement (NPP) and Request for Information (RFI) Green Procurement - Environmentally Preferable Packaging Public Works and Government Services Canada (PWGSC) is committed to supporting the procurement of environmentally preferable plastic products and the reduction of plastic packaging waste, and is taking concrete steps to better manage the use and disposal of plastics in its own operations as per the Policy on Green Procurement , the Greening Government Strategy: A Government of Canada Directive , and the Ocean Plastics Charter . Environmentally preferable packaging specifications are mandatory requirements in this procurement. Request for X (RFx); where X is Standing Offer, Supply Arrangement or Proposal Part 1 of RFx - Section 1.2 Statement of Work or Requirement The Offeror/Contractor must meet the mandatory environmentally preferable packaging specifications as indicated in the Statement of Work (SOW) or Statement of Requirement (SOR). The Offeror/Contractor must ensure that their recyclable packaging is accepted at the local recycling facilities of the delivery location(s) and must verify with the contracting authority whether their reusable packaging can be reused by the end user. The Offeror/Contractor will be responsible for taking back any packaging that cannot be recycled or reused by the end user at no additional cost to Canada. Statement of Work (SOW) or Statement of Requirement (SOR) In accordance with the Policy on Green Procurement and the Greening Government Strategy: A Government of Canada Directive , the Government of Canada is committed to supporting the procurement of environmentally preferable plastic products and the reduction of plastic packaging waste by integrating environmentally preferable packaging specifications in procurement and contract documents. All packaging material related to this requirement must be reusable, returnable or recyclable in accordance with the definitions set forth in Annex X . Excluded material * can also be found in Annex X . The Contractor is responsible for ensuring that their recyclable packaging is accepted at the local recycling facilities of the delivery location(s) and to verify with the contracting authority whether their reusable packaging can be reused by the end user. The Contractor must take back any packaging that cannot be recycled or reused by the end user at no additional cost to Canada.

Green Procurement - Environmentally Preferable Packaging

Public Works and Government Services Canada (PWGSC) is committed to supporting the procurement of environmentally preferable plastic products and the reduction of plastic packaging waste, and is taking concrete steps to better manage the use and disposal of plastics in its own operations as per the Policy on Green Procurement , the Greening Government Strategy: A Government of Canada Directive , and the Ocean Plastics Charter . Environmentally preferable packaging specifications are mandatory requirements in this procurement.

Part 1 of RFx - Section 1.2 Statement of Work or Requirement

The Offeror/Contractor must meet the mandatory environmentally preferable packaging specifications as indicated in the Statement of Work (SOW) or Statement of Requirement (SOR). The Offeror/Contractor must ensure that their recyclable packaging is accepted at the local recycling facilities of the delivery location(s) and must verify with the contracting authority whether their reusable packaging can be reused by the end user. The Offeror/Contractor will be responsible for taking back any packaging that cannot be recycled or reused by the end user at no additional cost to Canada.

In accordance with the Policy on Green Procurement and the Greening Government Strategy: A Government of Canada Directive , the Government of Canada is committed to supporting the procurement of environmentally preferable plastic products and the reduction of plastic packaging waste by integrating environmentally preferable packaging specifications in procurement and contract documents. All packaging material related to this requirement must be reusable, returnable or recyclable in accordance with the definitions set forth in Annex X . Excluded material * can also be found in Annex X . The Contractor is responsible for ensuring that their recyclable packaging is accepted at the local recycling facilities of the delivery location(s) and to verify with the contracting authority whether their reusable packaging can be reused by the end user. The Contractor must take back any packaging that cannot be recycled or reused by the end user at no additional cost to Canada.

Option 2: Optional requirements language

Use Option 2 for requirements where:

the delivered products require specialized packaging

products are to be delivered in an area with limited recycling options

adding a requirement for environmentally preferable packaging could be a major barrier to adequate supplier participation, to accessibility for persons with disabilities, or a risk to health, safety or national security

For implementation questions, see Frequently asked questions (FAQ) regarding standard language (accessible only on the Government of Canada network).

Table example with colour Procurement document Language to integrate into procurement documents Notice of Proposed Procurement (NPP) and Request for Information (RFI) Green Procurement - Environmentally Preferable Packaging Public Works and Government Services Canada (PWGSC) is committed to promoting the procurement of environmentally preferable plastic products and the reduction of plastic packaging waste as per the Policy on Green Procurement , the Greening Government Strategy: A Government of Canada Directive , and the Ocean Plastics Charter . Offerors/Contractors are encouraged to use environmentally preferable packaging for all packaged goods to be delivered to the end user as part of this procurement. Request for X (RFx); where X is Standing Offer, Supply Arrangement or Proposal Part 1 of RFx - Section 1.2 Statement of Work or Requirement The Offeror/Contractor should meet the environmentally preferable packaging specifications for this procurement, as indicated in the Statement of Work (SOW) or Statement of Requirement (SOR). The Offeror/Contractor should ensure that their recyclable packaging is accepted at the local recycling facilities of the delivery location(s) and should verify with the contracting authority whether their reusable packaging can be reused by the end user. The Offeror/Contractor should be responsible for taking back any packaging that cannot be recycled or reused by the end user at no additional cost to Canada. Statement of Work (SOW) or Statement of Requirement (SOR) In accordance with the Policy on Green Procurement and the Greening Government Strategy: A Government of Canada Directive , the Government of Canada is committed to promoting the procurement of environmentally preferable plastic products and the reduction of plastic packaging waste by considering environmentally preferable packaging specifications in procurement and contracts. All packaging material related to this requirement should be reusable, returnable or recyclable in accordance with the definitions set forth in Annex X . Excluded material * can also be found in Annex X . The Contractor is responsible for ensuring that their recyclable packaging is accepted at the local recycling facilities of the delivery location(s) and to verify with the contracting authority whether their reusable packaging can be reused by the end user. The Contractor must take back any packaging that cannot be recycled or reused by the end user at no additional cost to Canada.

Green Procurement - Environmentally Preferable Packaging

Public Works and Government Services Canada (PWGSC) is committed to promoting the procurement of environmentally preferable plastic products and the reduction of plastic packaging waste as per the Policy on Green Procurement , the Greening Government Strategy: A Government of Canada Directive , and the Ocean Plastics Charter . Offerors/Contractors are encouraged to use environmentally preferable packaging for all packaged goods to be delivered to the end user as part of this procurement.

Part 1 of RFx - Section 1.2 Statement of Work or Requirement

The Offeror/Contractor should meet the environmentally preferable packaging specifications for this procurement, as indicated in the Statement of Work (SOW) or Statement of Requirement (SOR). The Offeror/Contractor should ensure that their recyclable packaging is accepted at the local recycling facilities of the delivery location(s) and should verify with the contracting authority whether their reusable packaging can be reused by the end user. The Offeror/Contractor should be responsible for taking back any packaging that cannot be recycled or reused by the end user at no additional cost to Canada.

In accordance with the Policy on Green Procurement and the Greening Government Strategy: A Government of Canada Directive , the Government of Canada is committed to promoting the procurement of environmentally preferable plastic products and the reduction of plastic packaging waste by considering environmentally preferable packaging specifications in procurement and contracts. All packaging material related to this requirement should be reusable, returnable or recyclable in accordance with the definitions set forth in Annex X . Excluded material * can also be found in Annex X . The Contractor is responsible for ensuring that their recyclable packaging is accepted at the local recycling facilities of the delivery location(s) and to verify with the contracting authority whether their reusable packaging can be reused by the end user. The Contractor must take back any packaging that cannot be recycled or reused by the end user at no additional cost to Canada.

* Note on Excluded material: Environmentally preferable material alternatives for packaging tape are not widely available. As a result, packaging tape is excluded from the environmentally preferable packaging specifications until the market has progressed and studies become available to determine otherwise.

Green procurement evaluation indicators

Environmental evaluation indicators play a crucial role in developing evaluation criteria for green procurement. These indicators include:

Environmental certification, as follows:

certification through an independent program like the Environmental Choice Program or Energy Star

studies on the environmental attributes of the good or service

presence of a certified Environmental Product Declaration by bodies like the Canadian Standards Association or Underwriters laboratories of Canada (UL Solutions) or another accredited certification body

Energy and resource efficiency, as follows:

use of used, remanufactured, rebuilt or refurbished goods and/or materials

efficient resource and energy use throughout the product lifecycle

waste reduction capabilities

features that save energy, water or fuel, such as Power Down Mode

measures to extend the product's life, such as reuse, refill, recharge or recondition

Recycled content: inclusion of post-consumer recycled content and the types and percentages of recycled materials used.

Hazardous replacement, as follows:

availability of non-hazardous alternatives or replacements

requirement for Safety Data Sheets

Performance testing, as follows:

feasibility of pre-purchase testing

compliance with performance specifications

availability of documented past performance, such as environmental performance reports

Packaging, as follows:

inclusion of language for environmentally preferable packaging in the solicitation

minimal packaging to ensure safe delivery

options for bulk or concentrated form purchase

offeror removal of packaging post-installation

use of reusable, recyclable packaging materials

returnable to the contractor, or recyclable at delivery location(s)

reduction or elimination of single-use plastics

On-site waste management, as follows:

source separation and recycling of waste on-site

use of environmentally certified haulers and sites

design of goods or services to minimize waste, such as using biodegradable or reusable dishes

Return for disassembly and recycling, as follows:

manufacturer policies for product return for recycling

local recycling capabilities for the product

acceptance of consumables like toner cartridges for recycling

ease of disassembly

Warranties, as follows:

expected lifespan of the product

warranty duration and the advisability of purchasing extended warranties

Maintenance, as follows:

design conducive to easy maintenance and repair

availability of reasonably priced maintenance and replacement parts

ease and cost-effectiveness of upgrades

Environmental attributes of the offeror, as follows:

environmental certifications or registrations, such as ISO 14001

history of regulatory environmental compliance.

participation in a greenhouse gas (GHG) emission target-setting, reduction and disclosure programs like the GHG emission Net-Zero Challenge

Disclosure of greenhouse gas emissions and the setting of reduction targets

You must ensure that any procurement over $25 million, including taxes and set-asides induces suppliers to measure and report their greenhouse gas emissions and set a science-based target to cut emissions. This includes contracts, standing offers, supply arrangements, and call-ups. When the Standard on the Disclosure of Greenhouse Gas Emissions and the Setting of Reduction Targets applies, include this language in the solicitation:

“This procurement is subject to the Standard on the Disclosure of Greenhouse Gas Emissions and the Setting of Reduction Targets.”

You should also consider applying the standard for procurements between $1 million and $25 million, based on market readiness and client input.

There are five ways to apply the standard, depending on how ready the market is:

Mandatory or point-rated criteria

Certification before contract award

Conditionally limited solicitation

Contract clause requiring future compliance

Deliverable in the statement of work

For procurements under standing offers and supply arrangements over $25 million, you must choose a pathway and include it at the appropriate stage. For contracts under $5 million issued under these instruments, the standard can be excluded, and no exemption is needed.

Amend contracts issued before April 1, 2023, that exceed $25 million and have three or more years remaining to incorporate the standard.

You can request an exemption from the standard if there's a risk of no offers or if it's not feasible. Exemptions must be approved by the responsible senior official and recorded in the file.

Offerors can show compliance by participating in the Net-Zero Challenge or an equivalent initiative .

like the GHG emission Net-Zero Challenge Disclosure of greenhouse gas emissions and the setting of reduction targets You must ensure that any procurement over $25 million, including taxes and set-asides induces suppliers to measure and report their greenhouse gas emissions and set a science-based target to cut emissions. This includes contracts, standing offers, supply arrangements, and call-ups. When the Standard on the Disclosure of Greenhouse Gas Emissions and the Setting of Reduction Targets applies, include this language in the solicitation: “This procurement is subject to the Standard on the Disclosure of Greenhouse Gas Emissions and the Setting of Reduction Targets.” You should also consider applying the standard for procurements between $1 million and $25 million, based on market readiness and client input. There are five ways to apply the standard, depending on how ready the market is: Mandatory or point-rated criteria Certification before contract award Conditionally limited solicitation Contract clause requiring future compliance Deliverable in the statement of work For procurements under standing offers and supply arrangements over $25 million, you must choose a pathway and include it at the appropriate stage. For contracts under $5 million issued under these instruments, the standard can be excluded, and no exemption is needed. Amend contracts issued before April 1, 2023, that exceed $25 million and have three or more years remaining to incorporate the standard. You can request an exemption from the standard if there's a risk of no offers or if it's not feasible. Exemptions must be approved by the responsible senior official and recorded in the file. Offerors can show compliance by participating in the Net-Zero Challenge or an equivalent initiative .

Green procurement

The greening aspects that you must take into consideration throughout the procurement process are described as follows.

Life cycle costing

Life cycle costing pertains to all four stages of the procurement process: planning, acquisition, utilization, and disposal of goods and services. The Policy on Green Procurement applies to all federal government procurement activities. It requires that environmental performance considerations be embedded in procurement decisions, in the same manner as price, performance, quality and availability.

Standard language for environmentally preferable packaging

In order to meet our federal commitments to a more sustainable approach to producing, using and managing plastics, including reducing plastic waste and marine litter, PSPC is taking concrete steps to address packaging waste in procurement.

For the procurement of goods that come in packaging, buyers must include standard language on environmentally preferable packaging. Please refer to the table below to select the appropriate options to integrate into your procurement.

Option 1: Mandatory requirements language

This is the recommended option. Use this option when products are delivered in commercially available packaging. For implementation questions, see Frequently asked questions (FAQ) regarding standard language (accessible only on the Government of Canada network).

Table example with colour Procurement document Language to integrate into procurement documents Notice of Proposed Procurement (NPP) and Request for Information (RFI) Green Procurement - Environmentally Preferable Packaging Public Works and Government Services Canada (PWGSC) is committed to supporting the procurement of environmentally preferable plastic products and the reduction of plastic packaging waste, and is taking concrete steps to better manage the use and disposal of plastics in its own operations as per the Policy on Green Procurement , the Greening Government Strategy: A Government of Canada Directive , and the Ocean Plastics Charter . Environmentally preferable packaging specifications are mandatory requirements in this procurement. Request for X (RFx); where X is Standing Offer, Supply Arrangement or Proposal Part 1 of RFx - Section 1.2 Statement of Work or Requirement The Offeror/Contractor must meet the mandatory environmentally preferable packaging specifications as indicated in the Statement of Work (SOW) or Statement of Requirement (SOR). The Offeror/Contractor must ensure that their recyclable packaging is accepted at the local recycling facilities of the delivery location(s) and must verify with the contracting authority whether their reusable packaging can be reused by the end user. The Offeror/Contractor will be responsible for taking back any packaging that cannot be recycled or reused by the end user at no additional cost to Canada. Statement of Work (SOW) or Statement of Requirement (SOR) In accordance with the Policy on Green Procurement and the Greening Government Strategy: A Government of Canada Directive , the Government of Canada is committed to supporting the procurement of environmentally preferable plastic products and the reduction of plastic packaging waste by integrating environmentally preferable packaging specifications in procurement and contract documents. All packaging material related to this requirement must be reusable, returnable or recyclable in accordance with the definitions set forth in Annex X . Excluded material * can also be found in Annex X . The Contractor is responsible for ensuring that their recyclable packaging is accepted at the local recycling facilities of the delivery location(s) and to verify with the contracting authority whether their reusable packaging can be reused by the end user. The Contractor must take back any packaging that cannot be recycled or reused by the end user at no additional cost to Canada.

Green Procurement - Environmentally Preferable Packaging

Environmentally preferable packaging specifications are mandatory requirements in this procurement.

Part 1 of RFx - Section 1.2 Statement of Work or Requirement

The Offeror/Contractor must meet the mandatory environmentally preferable packaging specifications as indicated in the Statement of Work (SOW) or Statement of Requirement (SOR). The Offeror/Contractor must ensure that their recyclable packaging is accepted at the local recycling facilities of the delivery location(s) and must verify with the contracting authority whether their reusable packaging can be reused by the end user. The Offeror/Contractor will be responsible for taking back any packaging that cannot be recycled or reused by the end user at no additional cost to Canada.

In accordance with the Policy on Green Procurement and the Greening Government Strategy: A Government of Canada Directive , the Government of Canada is committed to supporting the procurement of environmentally preferable plastic products and the reduction of plastic packaging waste by integrating environmentally preferable packaging specifications in procurement and contract documents. All packaging material related to this requirement must be reusable, returnable or recyclable in accordance with the definitions set forth in Annex X . Excluded material * can also be found in Annex X . The Contractor is responsible for ensuring that their recyclable packaging is accepted at the local recycling facilities of the delivery location(s) and to verify with the contracting authority whether their reusable packaging can be reused by the end user. The Contractor must take back any packaging that cannot be recycled or reused by the end user at no additional cost to Canada.

Option 2: Optional requirements language

Use Option 2 for requirements where:

the delivered products require specialized packaging

products are to be delivered in an area with limited recycling options

adding a requirement for environmentally preferable packaging could be a major barrier to adequate supplier participation, to accessibility for persons with disabilities, or a risk to health, safety or national security

For implementation questions, see Frequently asked questions (FAQ) regarding standard language (accessible only on the Government of Canada network).

Green Procurement - Environmentally Preferable Packaging

Part 1 of RFx - Section 1.2 Statement of Work or Requirement

The Offeror/Contractor should meet the environmentally preferable packaging specifications for this procurement, as indicated in the Statement of Work (SOW) or Statement of Requirement (SOR). The Offeror/Contractor should ensure that their recyclable packaging is accepted at the local recycling facilities of the delivery location(s) and should verify with the contracting authority whether their reusable packaging can be reused by the end user. The Offeror/Contractor should be responsible for taking back any packaging that cannot be recycled or reused by the end user at no additional cost to Canada.

In accordance with the Policy on Green Procurement and the Greening Government Strategy: A Government of Canada Directive , the Government of Canada is committed to promoting the procurement of environmentally preferable plastic products and the reduction of plastic packaging waste by considering environmentally preferable packaging specifications in procurement and contracts. All packaging material related to this requirement should be reusable, returnable or recyclable in accordance with the definitions set forth in Annex X . Excluded material * can also be found in Annex X . The Contractor is responsible for ensuring that their recyclable packaging is accepted at the local recycling facilities of the delivery location(s) and to verify with the contracting authority whether their reusable packaging can be reused by the end user. * Note on Excluded material: Environmentally preferable material alternatives for packaging tape are not widely available. As a result, packaging tape is excluded from the environmentally preferable packaging specifications until the market has progressed and studies become available to determine otherwise.

Green procurement evaluation indicators

Environmental evaluation indicators play a crucial role in developing evaluation criteria for green procurement. These indicators include:

Environmental certification, as follows:

certification through an independent program like the Environmental Choice Program or Energy Star

studies on the environmental attributes of the good or service

presence of a certified Environmental Product Declaration by bodies like the Canadian Standards Association or Underwriters laboratories of Canada (UL Solutions) or another accredited certification body

Energy and resource efficiency, as follows:

use of used, remanufactured, rebuilt or refurbished goods and/or materials

efficient resource and energy use throughout the product lifecycle

waste reduction capabilities

features that save energy, water or fuel, such as Power Down Mode

measures to extend the product's life, such as reuse, refill, recharge or recondition

Recycled content: inclusion of post-consumer recycled content and the types and percentages of recycled materials used.

Hazardous replacement, as follows:

availability of non-hazardous alternatives or replacements

requirement for Safety Data Sheets

Performance testing, as follows:

feasibility of pre-purchase testing

compliance with performance specifications

availability of documented past performance, such as environmental performance reports

Packaging, as follows:

inclusion of language for environmentally preferable packaging in the solicitation

minimal packaging to ensure safe delivery

options for bulk or concentrated form purchase

offeror removal of packaging post-installation

use of reusable, recyclable packaging materials

returnable to the contractor, or recyclable at delivery location(s)

reduction or elimination of single-use plastics

On-site waste management, as follows:

source separation and recycling of waste on-site

use of environmentally certified haulers and sites

design of goods or services to minimize waste, such as using biodegradable or reusable dishes

Return for disassembly and recycling, as follows:

manufacturer policies for product return for recycling

local recycling capabilities for the product

acceptance of consumables like toner cartridges for recycling

ease of disassembly

Warranties, as follows:

expected lifespan of the product

warranty duration and the advisability of purchasing extended warranties

Maintenance, as follows:

design conducive to easy maintenance and repair

availability of reasonably priced maintenance and replacement parts

ease and cost-effectiveness of upgrades

Environmental attributes of the offeror, as follows:

environmental certifications or registrations, such as ISO 14001

history of regulatory environmental compliance.

participation in a greenhouse gas (GHG) emission target-setting, reduction and disclosure programs like the GHG emission Net-Zero Challenge

Disclosure of greenhouse gas emissions and the setting of reduction targets

You must ensure that any procurement over $25 million, including taxes and set-asides induces suppliers to measure and report their greenhouse gas emissions and set a science-based target to cut emissions. This includes contracts, standing offers, supply arrangements, and call-ups. When the Standard on the Disclosure of Greenhouse Gas Emissions and the Setting of Reduction Targets applies, include this language in the solicitation:

“This procurement is subject to the Standard on the Disclosure of Greenhouse Gas Emissions and the Setting of Reduction Targets.”

You should also consider applying the standard for procurements between $1 million and $25 million, based on market readiness and client input.

There are five ways to apply the standard, depending on how ready the market is:

Mandatory or point-rated criteria

Certification before contract award

Conditionally limited solicitation

Contract clause requiring future compliance

Deliverable in the statement of work

For procurements under standing offers and supply arrangements over $25 million, you must choose a pathway and include it at the appropriate stage. For contracts under $5 million issued under these instruments, the standard can be excluded, and no exemption is needed.

Amend contracts issued before April 1, 2023, that exceed $25 million and have three or more years remaining to incorporate the standard.

You can request an exemption from the standard if there's a risk of no offers or if it's not feasible. Exemptions must be approved by the responsible senior official and recorded in the file.

Offerors can show compliance by participating in the Net-Zero Challenge or an equivalent initiative .
